comments.text: Does anyone have a guess at a correct statement? The problem seems false for any $n, r$. Here's a GPT-5.2 construction: Fix an integer $r\ge 2$. For infinitely many $n$ (namely $n\equiv 1$ or $3 \pmod 6$) there exists a Steiner triple system on $n$ points, equivalently a decomposition of the edges of $K_n$ into edge-disjoint triangles. Fix such an $n$, and write\[ t=\frac{\binom{n}{2}}{3}=\frac{n(n-1)}6 \]for the number of triangles in this decomposition. Let $N$ be a parameter and consider the complete graph $K_N$ on vertex set $[N]:=\{1,\dots,N\}$. Colour each edge of $K_N$ independently and uniformly at random with one of the colours $1,\dots,r$. Fix an $n$-subset $S\subseteq [N]$ and a colour $c\in[r]$. Let $A_{S,c}$ be the event that $S$ contains no copy of $K_3$ in colour $c$ (i.e. the colour-$c$ subgraph induced by $S$ is triangle-free). Identifying $S$ with $\{1,\dots,n\}$, let $T_1,\dots,T_t$ be the $t$ edge-disjoint triangles given by the Steiner triple system. If $A_{S,c}$ holds then, in particular, none of the triangles $T_i$ is monochromatic of colour $c$. Since the $T_i$ are edge-disjoint and edge colours are independent, the events “$T_i$ is monochromatic of colour $c$” are independent, and for each $i$,\[ \mathbb P(T_i\text{ is monochromatic of colour }c)=r^{-3}. \]Hence\[ \mathbb P(A_{S,c}) \le \mathbb P(\forall i,\ T_i \text{ is not monochromatic of colour }c) =(1-r^{-3})^{\,t}. \]Call $S$ bad if $A_{S,c}$ holds for at least one colour $c\in[r]$; this is exactly the kind of $n$-set whose existence the definition of $R(n;3,r)$ would force. By a union bound over $c$,\[ \mathbb P(S\text{ is bad})\le \sum_{c=1}^r \mathbb P(A_{S,c})\le r(1-r^{-3})^{\,t}. \]Let $B$ be the number of bad $n$-subsets of $[N]$. By linearity of expectation and $\binom{N}{n}\le (eN/n)^n$,\[ \mathbb E[B]\le \binom{N}{n}\, r(1-r^{-3})^{\,t}\le \Bigl(\frac{eN}{n}\Bigr)^n\, r(1-r^{-3})^{\,t}. \]Choose $N=\lfloor e^{\gamma n}\rfloor$ with $\gamma<\frac16\log\!\bigl(\frac1{1-r^{-3}}\bigr)$. Then\[ \log \mathbb E[B]\le n\log\Bigl(\frac{eN}{n}\Bigr)+\log r+t\log(1-r^{-3}) =\Bigl(\gamma-\frac16\log\!\Bigl(\frac1{1-r^{-3}}\Bigr)\Bigr)n^2+o(n^2), \]so $\mathbb E[B]<1$ for all sufficiently large admissible $n$. Therefore there exists an $r$-colouring of $E(K_N)$ with $B=0$, i.e. every $n$-subset $S$ contains a monochromatic triangle in every colour. For these $n$ we have $R(n;3,r)>N\ge e^{c_r n}$ for some constant $c_r>0$ (e.g. any $c_r<\frac16\log(\frac1{1-r^{-3}})$). This contradicts any asserted upper bound $R(n;3,r)<C(r)^{\sqrt n}$, since $\exp(c_r n)$ eventually dominates $\exp((\log C(r))\sqrt n)$ as $n\to\infty$. Thus, under the stated definition of $R(n;3,r)$, the inequality $R(n;3,r)<C(r)^{\sqrt n}$ is false as written.
Yes, this is exactly the objection/construction already discussed in the remarks. (So is not a GPT construction.) I have no further ideas what was intended.
yeah this problem still puzzles me.
